title: Binary Search
code: public int Search(int[] nums, int target)

frames.caption: Initialize the closed interval: left = 0, right = 5. The answer, if any, lies in [left, right].
frames.note: Binary search needs two ingredients: an ordered domain and a yes/no test that is monotonic over it. Here the order is the sorted array and the test is "nums[mid] vs target". The closed interval [left, right] holds every index still in play, and the loop invariant is: if the target exists, it lives inside [left, right]. Keep that invariant true each step and the search is guaranteed to converge.

frames.caption: mid = 0 + (5 - 0)/2 = 2. Probe nums[2] = 5.
frames.note: Always compute the midpoint as left + (right − left) / 2, never (left + right) / 2 — for large indices the naive sum can overflow a 32-bit int (a famous bug that hid in Java's library for years). This single probe is the whole idea: one comparison of nums[mid] against target lets us throw away half of the remaining candidates in O(1), which is why the search costs only O(log n) comparisons.

frames.caption: nums[2] = 5 < 9: the target must be to the right. Discard the left half: left = mid + 1 = 3.
frames.note: Because the array is sorted, every element at or to the left of mid is ≤ nums[mid], which is already < target — the target cannot possibly be there, so discarding that whole half is safe. Setting left = mid + 1 (past mid, not back to mid) both excludes the element we just checked and guarantees the interval strictly shrinks, which is exactly what makes the loop terminate instead of spinning forever.

frames.caption: mid = 3 + (5 - 3)/2 = 4. Probe nums[4] = 9.
frames.note: Always compute the midpoint as left + (right − left) / 2, never (left + right) / 2 — for large indices the naive sum can overflow a 32-bit int (a famous bug that hid in Java's library for years). This single probe is the whole idea: one comparison of nums[mid] against target lets us throw away half of the remaining candidates in O(1), which is why the search costs only O(log n) comparisons.

frames.caption: nums[4] = 9 == target. Found it — return index 4.
frames.note: Equality is the win condition, so we return the index immediately. Notice the loop test is left <= right, not left < right: that <= is what lets a one-element interval (where left == right) still be probed. Use < here and you would skip that final candidate and silently miss answers that sit at the boundary.
